questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of Mobitz I second degree AV block?
questions.explanation: Mobitz I (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block is caused by fatigue or progressive exhaustion of the atrioventricular node, which lengthens the PR interval until a P wave is no longer conducted.

questions.question: What electrocardiographic finding characterizes Mobitz I second-degree AV block?
options: P waves and QRS completely dissociated
Fixed PR interval and blocked P wave
Short PR interval and delta wave
Progressive lengthening of the PR until blockage
questions.explanation: Mobitz I (Wenckebach) AV block is characterized by a progressive lengthening of the PR interval until a P wave is not conducted into the ventricle, followed by a pause.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of Mobitz I second degree AV block?
questions.explanation: Mobitz I AV block is frequently associated with acute inferior myocardial infarction, due to ischemic involvement of the atrioventricular node, which is usually reversible.

questions.question: What initial treatment is indicated in an asymptomatic patient with Mobitz I AV block?
questions.explanation: Mobitz I AV block in asymptomatic patients does not require immediate treatment; Observation and management of the underlying cause is recommended, as it is usually benign and reversible.

questions.question: What pattern of R-R intervals is typically seen in Mobitz I AV block?
questions.explanation: In Mobitz I AV block, the R-R intervals are progressively shortened due to PR lengthening, until pause occurs due to the unconducted P wave.

questions.question: What key difference distinguishes the Mobitz I AV Block from the Mobitz II?
options: Constant PR interval in Mobitz II
questions.explanation: In Mobitz II AV block the PR interval remains constant before the blocked P wave, while in Mobitz I there is progressive lengthening of the PR.

questions.question: What is the characteristic electrocardiographic finding of Mobitz II second degree AV block?
questions.explanation: In second-degree Mobitz II AV block, the PR interval remains constant before the unconducted P wave, and the QRS is usually wide due to infra-Hisian block.

questions.question: Where is the lesion typically located in Mobitz II second degree AV block?
questions.explanation: Mobitz II block is infra-Hisian, located in the His-Purkinje system, which explains the wide QRS frequency and the progression to complete block.

questions.question: What is the initial therapeutic approach in a symptomatic patient with Mobitz II AV block?
questions.explanation: Symptomatic Mobitz II block has a high risk of progression to complete block; A temporary pacemaker is indicated as an urgent measure before the definitive one.

questions.question: What medication is contraindicated in a patient with Mobitz II AV block?
questions.explanation: Verapamil, a calcium channel blocker, can depress AV conduction and worsen Mobitz II block, so it is contraindicated.

questions.question: What differentiates Mobitz II AV block from first degree AV block?
questions.explanation: In first-degree AV block all P waves are conducted with elongated PR; in Mobitz II there are intermittently unconducted P waves.

questions.question: What is the main risk of untreated Mobitz II AV block?
questions.explanation: Mobitz II block usually progresses to complete third-degree AV block, causing severe bradycardia, syncope, and cardiac arrest if a pacemaker is not implanted.

questions.question: What electrocardiographic characteristic defines third degree AV block?
options: Unrelated P waves and QRS complexes
Hidden P waves in the QRS
Fixed PR interval greater than 0.20 sec
questions.explanation: In third degree AV block there is complete atrioventricular dissociation: the P waves (atrial activity) and the QRS complexes (ventricular activity) are independent, unrelated to each other.

questions.question: What is the typical ventricular rate in third-degree AV block with ventricular escape rhythm?
questions.explanation: In third degree AV block the ventricular escape rhythm usually originates in the bundle of His or more distal, generating a low ventricular rate, generally between 20 and 40 beats per minute.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of acquired third degree AV block?
questions.explanation: Ischemic heart disease, especially acute inferior myocardial infarction, is the most common cause of acquired third-degree AV block, due to involvement of the AV node or conduction system.

questions.question: What classic clinical finding is associated with third degree AV block?
questions.explanation: Pulsus alternans, characterized by an alternation in the amplitude of the arterial pulse, is classically observed in third-degree AV block due to the variation in ventricular filling associated with atrioventricular dissociation.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for symptomatic third-degree AV block?
questions.explanation: The definitive treatment of symptomatic third-degree AV block is the implantation of a permanent cardiac pacemaker. Atropine and isoproterenol are temporary measures, not curative.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism explains atrioventricular dissociation in third degree AV block?
options: Increased vagal tone over the AV node
Complete blockage at the sinus node
Atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response
questions.explanation: In third degree AV block there is a complete interruption of impulse conduction from the atria to the ventricles, so no supraventricular stimulus is able to activate the ventricles.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of acquired atrioventricular block?
options: Fibrous degeneration of the conduction system
questions.explanation: Idiopathic fibrous degeneration of the conduction system (Lenègre or Lev disease) is the most common cause of acquired AV block in older adults, surpassing inflammatory or ischemic causes.

questions.question: What anatomical structure is primarily affected in conduction system degeneration that causes AV block?
options: Atrioventricular node and bundle of His
questions.explanation: Fibrous degeneration typically affects the AV node, the bundle of His, or its proximal branches, interrupting supraventricular conduction. The sinus node and distal fibers are usually spared.

questions.question: Which of the following factors is most frequently associated with degeneration of the conduction system?
questions.explanation: Aging is the main factor associated with fibrous degeneration of the conduction system. Hypertension and diabetes are comorbidities, but their direct association as a cause is less than age.

questions.question: What type of AV block is usually the first manifestation of conduction system degeneration?
options: Mobitz I second degree AV block
Mobitz II second degree AV block
questions.explanation: First-degree AV block (PR interval prolongation) is often the initial manifestation of fibrous degeneration, before progressing to more advanced degrees. Mobitz II and third degree are late stages.

questions.question: What is the definitive treatment of symptomatic AV block due to degeneration of the conduction system?
questions.explanation: The definitive pacemaker is the only curative treatment for symptomatic AV block due to fibrous degeneration, since there is no pharmacological therapy that reverses the structural damage. Atropine and isoproterenol are temporary measures.

questions.question: Which electrocardiographic finding is characteristic of late-stage conduction system degeneration?
options: Wide QRS complexes with AV dissociation
Short PR interval and delta wave
Narrow QRS complexes with AV dissociation
questions.explanation: In advanced degeneration, complete AV block usually presents with a ventricular escape rhythm with a wide QRS (>120 ms), since the underlying pacemaker originates distal to the bundle of His. Narrow QRS would suggest nodal block.

questions.question: What type of AV block is most common in inferior infarction?
options: Mobitz II second degree AV block
questions.explanation: In inferior infarction, first-degree AV block is the most common due to ischemic involvement of the AV node and its rich irrigation by the right coronary artery.

questions.question: What is the pathophysiological mechanism of AV block in inferior infarction?
options: Fibrosis of the bundle of His
questions.explanation: In inferior infarction, AV node ischemia occurs due to occlusion of the right coronary artery, which supplies the AV node in most patients.

questions.question: What electrocardiographic finding is typical of AV block in anterior infarction?
options: Hidden P wave in QRS complex
Nonconducted P wave with narrow QRS
Nonconducted P wave with wide QRS
questions.explanation: AV block in anterior infarction is usually infranodal, with wide QRS and unconducted P waves, due to necrosis of the distal conduction system.

questions.question: What complication is most likely in AV block due to anterior infarction?
questions.explanation: AV block in anterior infarction is more prone to syncope and cardiac arrest due to involvement of the His-Purkinje system, unlike inferior infarction.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for complete AV block in inferior infarction?
questions.explanation: Atropine is the initial treatment of complete AV block in inferior infarction due to its vagal origin, reversing the nodal block in many cases.

questions.question: What characteristic differentiates AV block from inferior to anterior infarction?
questions.explanation: In inferior infarction, AV block is usually nodal with a narrow QRS and benign course, while in the anterior it is infranodal with a wide QRS and poor prognosis.

questions.question: What is the main indication for definitive pacemaker in AV block?
questions.explanation: The definitive pacemaker is indicated in symptomatic high-grade AV block (advanced grade III or II) to relieve symptoms and prevent syncope or sudden death.

questions.question: What symptom is an indication for a pacemaker in AV block?
questions.explanation: Syncope is a serious symptom associated with high-grade AV block; Its presence constitutes a clear indication to implant a permanent pacemaker.

questions.question: What type of pacemaker is preferred in complete AV block?
questions.explanation: The dual-chamber pacemaker (DDD) maintains atrioventricular synchrony, improving cardiac output and reducing the risk of pacemaker syndrome in complete AV block.

questions.question: What electrocardiographic characteristic characterizes complete AV block?
options: P waves followed by fixed QRS
questions.explanation: In complete AV block (grade III) there is total atrioventricular dissociation: the atria and ventricles activate independently, with no relationship between P and QRS waves.

questions.question: What is an early complication of pacemaker implantation?
questions.explanation: Generator pocket infection is a common early complication (days to weeks) after implantation, requiring antibiotics and sometimes device removal.

questions.question: What drug is used temporarily before pacing in symptomatic AV block?
questions.explanation: Atropine can increase heart rate in nodal AV block (infranodal does not respond), being used as a temporary measure until a permanent pacemaker is placed.

questions.question: What is the main indication for pacing in high-grade AV block?
options: Second degree AV block type Mobitz I
Asymptomatic Mobitz II type second degree AV block
questions.explanation: Symptomatic third-degree AV block is a class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation, since it causes symptomatic bradycardia and risk of asystole.

questions.question: What type of AV block requires pacemaker even if the patient is asymptomatic?
options: Mobitz II type second degree AV block
Third degree AV block with escape rhythm
Second degree AV block type Mobitz I
questions.explanation: Second-degree AV block type Mobitz II has a risk of progression to complete block and asystole, which is why a pacemaker is indicated even in asymptomatic patients according to guidelines.

questions.question: What is the most common symptom that warrants a pacemaker in high-grade AV block?
questions.explanation: Syncope or presyncope is the most common symptom in high-grade AV block due to severe bradycardia, indicating the need for a pacemaker to prevent falls and sudden death.

questions.question: Which electrocardiographic finding indicates high-grade AV block?
questions.explanation: High-grade AV block is characterized by multiple consecutive unconducted P waves, with prolonged ventricular pauses that can cause hemodynamic instability.

questions.question: What is the typical ventricular escape rate in complete AV block?
questions.explanation: In complete AV block, the ventricular escape rate is usually between 40-60 bpm if it is from the AV junction, or slower if it is ventricular, insufficient to maintain cardiac output.

questions.question: What drug is contraindicated in high-grade AV block without a pacemaker?
questions.explanation: Beta-blockers such as atenolol depress AV conduction and can precipitate asystole in high-grade AV block, so they are contraindicated without backup pacemaker.

questions.question: What functional class of the Killip-Kimball scale defines post-MI high-grade AV block with peripheral hypoperfusion?
questions.explanation: Killip class IV corresponds to cardiogenic shock, characterized by peripheral hypoperfusion, hypotension, and signs of low cardiac output, which may accompany high-grade AV block after AMI.

questions.question: What is the immediate treatment of post-AMI third-degree AV block associated with Killip III?
questions.explanation: Transcutaneous pacing is the emergency intervention for high-grade AV block with hemodynamic instability (Killip III), since atropine is usually ineffective in infrahisian blocks.

questions.question: Which radiological finding is characteristic of Killip class II in a patient with post-MI AV block?
questions.explanation: Pulmonary vascular redistribution (hilar engorgement) is a radiological sign of mild to moderate pulmonary congestion, typical of Killip II, indicating incipient left heart failure.

questions.question: What is the main objective of the Killip-Kimball scale in post-AMI AV block?
questions.explanation: The Killip-Kimball scale classifies the severity of post-MI acute heart failure to stratify hemodynamic risk and guide urgent therapeutic decisions, such as the use of pacemakers.

questions.question: What physical sign defines Killip class I in a patient with post-AMI AV block?
questions.explanation: Killip class I is characterized by the absence of clinical signs of congestive heart failure (CHF), that is, without crackles, third heart sound, or jugular engorgement, indicating preserved ventricular function.

questions.question: Which of the following drugs is contraindicated in Killip IV with post-MI AV block?
questions.explanation: Intravenous metoprolol is contraindicated in cardiogenic shock (Killip IV) due to its negative inotropic and negative chronotropic effect, which can worsen AV block and hypoperfusion.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic AV block in digitalis poisoning?
options: Mobitz I second degree AV block
Mobitz II second degree AV block
questions.explanation: Digitalis intoxication typically produces second-degree AV block type Mobitz I (Wenckebach), due to the vagotonic and direct depressant effect on nodal conduction.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism predominates in digitalis AV block?
questions.explanation: Digitalis increases vagal tone, which prolongs the refractory period of the AV node and slows conduction, this being the main mechanism of AV block.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for symptomatic AV block due to digitalis?
questions.explanation: Atropine antagonizes excess vagal tone induced by digitalis and can reverse symptomatic AV block, being the first-line treatment before other measures.

questions.question: What electrocardiographic finding is typical in digitalis poisoning with AV block?
options: Inverted T waves with ST depression
questions.explanation: Digitalis poisoning classically shows scooped ST-segment depression and inverted or flattened T waves, along with AV block.

questions.question: What serum level of digoxin is considered toxic and associated with AV block?
questions.explanation: The therapeutic range of digoxin is 0.5-2.0 ng/mL; Levels greater than 2.0 ng/mL are considered toxic and can cause AV block and arrhythmias.

questions.question: What factor predisposes to digital AV block in patients with atrial fibrillation?
questions.explanation: Hypokalemia enhances digitalis toxicity by increasing the binding of the drug to the Na+/K+-ATPase pump, favoring the appearance of AV block and ventricular arrhythmias.
